coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of highly fluorescent, amine-functionalized carbon dots from biotin-modified chitosan and silk-fibroin blend for target-specific delivery of antitumor agents
coredata.dc:creator: Horo, Himali
Saha, Muktashree
Das, Himadree
Mandal, Bishnupada
Kundu, Lal Mohan
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have been a promising theranostic tool with high biocompatibility and a tailorable fluorescence profile. Herein, we report the synthesis of highly fluorescent amine-functionalized CDs from low molecular weight chitosan (LMWC) and silk-fibroin (SF) blends. The synthesized CDs were quasi-spherical in shape with a size of 3 ± 1.5 nm. A significant increase in fluorescent intensity and quantum yield was achieved upon increasing the SF content due to nitrogen doping. For inducing target specificity to cancer cells, biotin was covalently conjugated to the CDs, and the conjugation was determined by FTIR spectroscopy. The conjugate was further loaded with 5-fluorouracil (5-FU) as a model anti-cancer drug. The MTT assay showed increased cytotoxicity of the conjugated CDs in cancer cells compared to normal cells. The live-cell imaging in MCF-7 cell lines showed bright blue-colored fluorescence and increased internalization of the conjugated CDs than the non-conjugate ones due to receptor-mediated endocytosis.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of highly fluorescent, amine-functionalized carbon dots from biotin-modified chitosan and silk-fibroin blend for target-specific delivery of antitumor agents

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have been a promising theranostic tool with high biocompatibility and a tailorable fluorescence profile. Herein, we report the synthesis of highly fluorescent amine-functionalized CDs from low molecular weight chitosan (LMWC) and silk-fibroin (SF) blends. The synthesized CDs were quasi-spherical in shape with a size of 3 ± 1.5 nm. A significant increase in fluorescent intensity and quantum yield was achieved upon increasing the SF content due to nitrogen doping. For inducing target specificity to cancer cells, biotin was covalently conjugated to the CDs, and the conjugation was determined by FTIR spectroscopy. The conjugate was further loaded with 5-fluorouracil (5-FU) as a model anti-cancer drug. The MTT assay showed increased cytotoxicity of the conjugated CDs in cancer cells compared to normal cells. The live-cell imaging in MCF-7 cell lines showed bright blue-colored fluorescence and increased internalization of the conjugated CDs than the non-conjugate ones due to receptor-mediated endocytosis.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanotheranostics, which integrates diagnosis and therapy into a single platform, has emerged as one of the powerful strategies in cancer treatment. Smart, nano-sized particles that can potentially detect the presence of cancer cells and induce drug delivery to the targeted tumor cells are perceived as nano theranostics agents (Chen et al., 2017). Various types of luminescent multicomponent organic and inorganic nano-carriers have been developed, among which carbon dots (CDs) or carbon nanoparticles have been recognized as highly promising theranostic candidates. CDs are quasi-spherical, zero-dimensional particles with a size of less than 10 nm, thus proficient in penetrating the cells (Wang et al., 2017; Zhu et al., 2019). They often possess excellent photostability, biocompatibility, water-solubility, non-toxicity, and low production cost. The most notable feature of the CDs is that they exhibit tunable fluorescence profiles that may range from blue to near-infrared, making them a perfect choice for bioimaging, smart sensing systems, and bio-labeling (He et al., 2019; Jia et al., 2020).

There are two general methods for the synthesis of CDs, ‘top-down’ and ‘bottom-up’. In the ‘top-down’ approach, large carbon compounds are broken down into nano-sized materials using arc-discharge, laser ablation, electrochemical or chemical oxidation (Wang & Hu, 2014). However, the functionalization and purification steps are often time-consuming and intricate (Radwan-pragłowska & Bogdał, 2019). In the ‘bottom-up’ approach, organic molecules or biomass are carbonized at a higher temperature using pyrolysis, microwave, ultrasonication, hydrothermal, or plasma treatment (Tajik et al., 2020). ‘Bottom-up’ provides the provision for tailoring the composition and physical properties of the CDs by a wide selection of precursor and carbonizing parameters. Hence, it is mostly preferred over the ‘top-down’ approaches from a scientific and commercial point of view (Yang et al., 2013).

Depending on the types of precursor and synthesis route, various kinds of functional groups such as amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl appear on the surface of the CDs. These groups ensure water solubility and scope for further functionalization (Pawar et al., 2019). Biopolymers have enormous potential in developing innovative biomaterials, including CDs, due to their variable functional groups, biocompatibility, and high cell viability. Several cost-effective and environment-friendly processes have been adopted to synthesize CDs using biopolymers, proteins, and plant extracts (Gao et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2019a, 2019b; Moradi et al., 2018). Chitosan can be a suitable biopolymeric precursor for the synthesis of CDs as it possesses a large number of free hydroxyl and amino functional groups in its backbone. Chitosan is a natural linear amino polysaccharide prepared by alkaline deacetylation of chitin, the second most abundant natural polymer. It finds a wide range of biomedical applications in various forms due to its excellent biocompatibility, non-toxicity, mucoadhesiveness, and biodegradability (Bhattarai et al., 2010; Elbehairi et al., 2020; Herdiana et al., 2021; Sofy et al., 2019, 2020; Yu et al., 2021). Synthesis of chitosan-based amino-functionalized CDs has been reported by various researchers, which showed low-cytotoxicity and better stability. However, the CDs exhibited low photoluminescence efficiency (Liu et al., 2016; Pawar et al., 2019). Heteroatom doping, such as nitrogen doping, is an effective strategy to escalate quantum yield and photoluminescence efficiency (Liu et al., 2019a, 2019b). Silk fibroin, derived from Bombyx mori silk, is a protein with high nitrogen content and can be used as doping material (Wang et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2013). All the primary, secondary and tertiary amines can contribute to nitrogen doping, with primary amine being more effective (Qu et al., 2014). Furthermore, SF has found various notable applications in the biomedical field due to its high biocompatibility, tailorable degradation profile, ease in chemical modifications, aqueous processability, and abundant availability (Murphy & Kaplan, 2010; Qi et al., 2017; Rockwood et al., 2011).

Target specificity is the most significant aspect in designing an anti-cancer drug delivery tool. The main drawback of chemotherapy is the low bioavailability of the administered drugs and the side effects of killing the healthy cells (Wang et al., 2010). The CD's surface functional groups enable the attachment of the different drug targeting moieties via covalent conjugation. Nano-carriers conjugated with tumor-targeting moieties would direct the carrier to the affected cells, sparing the healthy cells (Kim & Nie, 2005). Some small molecules and short peptides have been discovered to show high specificity towards particular tumor cells. Biotin is one of the essential cell-targeting ligands that target the cancer cells, as biotin receptors are overexpressed in many cancer cells, more than the other cell-targeting ligand receptors such as folic acid and vitamin B12 (Zhao et al., 2010). Literature reports suggest that the covalent conjugation of the ligands does not seem to quench the fluorescence intensity of the CDs significantly; instead helps in accumulating the CDs in the cancer cells more than the normal cells. The fluorescence properties of conjugated CDs enabled the study of the internalization of the targeted systems into the cells (Mewada et al., 2014; Scialabba et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2017).

Herein, we have synthesized primary amine-functionalized CDs derived from LMWC and SF blend. The natural carbohydrate, LMWC, acts as the primary carbon source and amino-functionalizing precursor due to the large carbon content and free –NH2 groups on its backbone. On the other hand, the SF serves as the nitrogen source for doping owing to its high nitrogen content. The importance and effectiveness of SF as the doping material have been analyzed by evaluating the effect on fluorescence intensities and quantum yield upon increasing the SF content. The CDs were further covalently conjugated with biotin for inducing cancer cell specificity. Additionally, we have loaded 5-fluorouracil (5-FU) as a model chemotherapeutic drug to the biotin conjugated CDs. The system is expected to act as an effective nano theranostics tool with cell-targeting properties.

LMWC (poly (β-(1,4)-d-glucosamine), 94% deacetylated, 129 kDa viscosity average molecular weight) and 5-Fluorouracil (C4H3FN2O2, >99%) were procured from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, United States). Biotin (C10H16N2O3S, >98%) was purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry (Tokyo, Japan). Lithium bromide (LiBr, 99%) and N, N′-Dicyclohexylcarbodiimide, DCC (C13H22N2, >99%) were purchased from Spectrochem Pvt. Ltd. (Mumbai, India). Sodium carbonate (Na2CO3, ≥99) was procured from Merck (Mumbai, India) and N-Hyroxysuccinimide, NHS (C4H5NO3, ≥98%) from Alfa Aesar (Heysham, England). B. mori silk cocoons were acquired from local silk farms, Assam, India.

UV–Vis absorbance spectra were recorded using Agilent Carry 100 UV–Vis spectrophotometer. FTIR spectra were recorded on a Perkin Elmer Spectrum 2 FTIR spectrometer over a frequency range of 4000–400 cm−1 at room temperature using KBr. The size of the CDs was analyzed by Jeol 2100F Field Emission Transmission Electron Microscope (FETEM). For the analysis, a dilute CD droplet was cast on a copper grid and dried overnight in a desiccator. The fluorescence intensities were analyzed using 1 mg/ml concentration by Horiba Scientific Fluoromax-4 Spectrofluorometer. The thermal property of dried SF was determined by TGA-thermogravimetric analysis using STA7200 thermal analysis system, Hitachi High Technologies, Japan, at a heating rate of 10 °C/min from 25 to 700 °C under nitrogen atmosphere. The crystalline/amorphous property of the materials was determined by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) using Rigaku SmartLab 9 kW X-ray diffractometer fitted with CuKα radiation of λ = 1.54 Å in a 2θ range of 5–60°. The surface morphology and roughness of the material were analyzed by Bruker, Innova series atomic force microscopy (AFM) using TESPA-V2, Bruker antimony doped silicon tips.

LMWC was characterized for its degree of deacetylation (DD %) and viscosity average molecular weight (Mv), which we have reported earlier (Horo et al., 2019). The DD % was determined to be 94% by pH-metric titration and FTIR spectroscopy. The Mv was determined by viscometric measurement using Ubbelohde viscometer. The intrinsic viscosity, [Ƞ] obtained from the measurement was used to calculate the Mv using the Mark-Houwink-Sakurada equation, [Ƞ] = KMv α where K and α are the constants at a given temperature (30 °C) and solute-solvent (0.1 M CH3COONa/0.2 M CH3COOH) system. The Mv was calculated to be 1,29,882 Da. The SF was also characterized using TGA, powder XRD, and FTIR spectroscopy, which we have already reported (Horo et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction of SF from bombyx mori silk cocoons
ce:section.ce:para: The extraction of SF was done from bombyx mori silk cocoons following an established protocol (Rockwood et al., 2011). Briefly, the cocoons were cut into smaller pieces and boiled in 0.02 M Na2CO3 solution for 1 h. The sericin-free fibers were washed and dried overnight at room temperature. The dried fibers were then dissolved in LiBr solution at 70 °C for 4 h followed by dialysis against deionized water for 3 days. The solution was then centrifuged at 9000 rpm and 4 °C for 20 mins. The pure SF solution was dried and stored at 4 °C before use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots from LMWC and SF blend (CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs were synthesized by pyrolysis of LMWC and SF blend using five different ratios of LMWC to SF viz. 1:0, 1:0.66, 1:1, 1:1.5, and 0:1. The carbonization was carried out under a nitrogen atmosphere at 230 °C for 3 h. The obtained product was ground to a fine powder and dissolved in water. The solution was then filtered to discard the impurities, and the filtrate containing the dissolved CDs was collected, freeze-dried, and stored at 4 °C until further use.

The BT-NHS ester was synthesized using a reported protocol with minor modifications (Buller et al., 2011). Briefly, biotin (100 mg, 0.5 mmol) was dissolved in hot DMF at 60 °C until a clear solution was obtained. After cooling to room temperature, NHS (57.52 mg, 0.6 mmol) and DCC (84.38 mg, 0.5 mmol) were added, and the solution was stirred overnight at room temperature. The precipitated solid by-product (dicyclohexylurea) was filtered off, and the filtrate was concentrated using a rotary evaporator. The concentrated solution was precipitated using diethyl ether. The solid was recrystallized using 2-propanol and dried in a vacuum to yield a white solid product.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of biotin-conjugated carbon dots (BT-CDs) and 5-FU loading (FU- BT-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs (80 mg) were dissolved in 100 mM sodium bicarbonate buffer of pH 9.0. Biotin-NHS (160 mg) was dissolved in DMSO until a clear solution was obtained. The CDs solution was dropwise added to the Biotin-NHS solution and reacted overnight at room temperature. The reaction mixture was dialyzed against distilled water using a dialysis membrane of MW cutoff 1 kDa to remove the non-conjugated biotin. For loading the drug, 26 mg of 5-FU was incubated with 10 ml of BT-CDs solution at 37 °C for 24 h. The solution was then centrifuged at 16,000 rpm to collect the drug-loaded nanoparticle pellets, which were then stored at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Encapsulation efficiency (EE %) and drug loading efficiency (DL %) measurement
ce:section.ce:para:
The UV–Vis spectroscopic technique was used to determine the EE % and DL % of 5-FU in CDs. The amount of loaded 5-FU was obtained using an indirect method by analyzing the supernatant of the centrifuged FU-BT-CDs solution at 266 nm. The amount of free 5-FU was determined from the absorbance value by comparing it with the calibration curve of pure 5-FU. The EE % and DL % were then calculated using the following equations.
EE % =
Total drug added − free drug
Total drug added
× 100 %
DL % =
Total drug added − free drug
Weight of nanoparticle
× 100 %

The in vitro release of 5-FU from FU-BT-CDs was done by dissolving a known amount of material in a PBS buffer of pH 7.4. The solution was then transferred to a dialysis membrane of MW cutoff 1 kDa and immersed in 20 ml of PBS buffer maintained under constant stirring. At chosen time intervals, 500 μl aliquot of sample was taken out and replaced with the same quantity of fresh PBS buffer. The collected aliquots were analyzed spectrophotometrically at 266 nm, and the percentage of drug release was calculated using a standard calibration curve of pure 5-FU. The release study was done in triplicates, and the error was estimated from the standard deviation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell lines and cell culture conditions
ce:section.ce:para: MCF-7 (Human breast cancer cell lines), HeLa (Human cervical cancer cell lines), and HEK-296 (Human embryonic kidney cell lines) were acquired from National Centre for Cell Science (NCCS) repository Pune. The respective cell lines were cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) supplemented with 1% penicillin-streptomycin and 10% (v/v) fetal bovine serum (FBS). They were further incubated in a CO2 incubator at 37 °C humidified atmosphere having 5% CO2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of cytotoxicity by MTT assay
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of the synthesized materials was analyzed by MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5 diphenyl tetrazolium bromide) assay. All the cell lines were seeded in triplicates on 96-well tissue culture plates with a density of 5 × 103 cells/well and maintained overnight in a CO2 incubator for attachment. For comparative study, 5-FU loaded CDs were also synthesized without biotin (FU-CDs). The cells were then treated with increasing concentrations of (a) pure CDs, (b) pure 5-FU, (c) FU-CDs, and (d) FU- BT-CDs for 48 h. The treatment media was replaced with 0.5 mg/ml MTT solution dissolved in PBS buffer, and the cells were incubated for 2 h. Thereafter, DMSO was added to it, and the absorbance was recorded at 570 nm using a Tecan multiplate reader having a reference of 630 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Confocal imaging to study the cellular uptake of CDs and BT-CDs by cancer cell lines (MCF-7)
ce:section.ce:para: Cellular uptake of the CDs and BT-CDs by cancer cell lines (MCF-7) was studied by confocal imaging. MCF-7 cell lines were seeded in glass-bottom plates at a density of 1.5 × 105 cells per well. Cellular uptake of the CDs and BT-CDs was observed after incubating them with the cells for 24 h in a CO2 incubator for attachment. The cells were subsequently washed with PBS to remove any unbound particles and then fixed using 4% formaldehyde for 15 min, washed thrice with PBS, and visualized under a confocal laser scanning microscope (Zeiss LSM 880) at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm and an emission of 420 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Confocal imaging to study the cellular uptake of BT-CDs by cancer and normal cells
ce:section.ce:para: The specificity of the BT-CDs was investigated using confocal imaging of a cancer cell line (MCF-7) which has positive biotin expression and a non-cancerous cell line (HEK-293), which is biotin negative (−). The cells were seeded in glass-bottom plates at a density of 1.5 × 105 cells per well and incubated for 24 h. Subsequently, they were treated with BT-CDs (100 μg/ml) for 24 h. Thereafter, the cells were washed and visualized under a confocal laser scanning microscope following the same procedure as in 2.12.

The carbon dots were synthesized by the pyrolysis of two biopolymers, LMWC and SF, by varying their ratios. The LMWC is used as the carbon source and functionalizing precursor for the formation of CDs. The SF was used as the nitrogen source for nitrogen doping which increases the photoluminescence efficiency. The synthesized CDs possess free amino groups, which can be covalently conjugated to the free carboxyl group of biotin by amide coupling reactions. 5-FU is encapsulated on the BT-CDs, presumably through the interaction of the hydrophilic 5-FU with the free amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface of the CDs (Miralinaghi et al., 2019; Nivethaa et al., 2015). The overall synthesis process of the drug carrier is demonstrated in Scheme 1 . The binding interaction of 5-FU and BT-CDs was studied by isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) (supplementary data).

We have synthesized the ester form of biotin for conjugation because: (a) it is highly reactive; (b) it generates high yield in amine coupling reaction (Anderson et al., 1963); (c) it is resistant to hydrolysis, thus stable and can be stored for a more extended period (Klykov & Weller, 2015). The synthesis of biotin-NHS ester and its conjugation to CDs is represented in Scheme 2 . Biotin (1) reacts with N, N′-Dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (2) to form an O-acylisourea intermediate (3) after a nucleophilic attack of the carboxyl group of biotin on the carbodiimide carbon of DCC. The NHS (4) reacts with the O-acylisourea intermediate to form biotin-NHS (5) and a by-product dicyclohexylurea (6), which is insoluble in DMF and can be easily separated by filtration. Then (5) reacts with the free amino group of CDs (7) to yield BT-CDs (8).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of optical properties of CDs

ce:section.ce:para: The CDs exhibited a bright blue fluorescence upon exposure to UV-lamp, as shown in Fig. 1 (A), which is the primary indication of the formation of the CDs. Fig. S1 (supplementary data) displays the different ratios of CDs when analyzed under daylight and UV-lamp of 365 nm. They were colorless to light yellow in daylight depending upon the ratios of LMWC to SF, while they showed bright blue fluorescence upon excitation.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the CDs synthesized with different ratios of LMWC and SF are shown in Fig. 1(B). The UV–Vis spectrum of CDs synthesized from pure LMWC exhibits a single absorption band at 254 nm, which can be ascribed to the π-π* transition of the CC group of the CDs. While the UV–Vis spectra of the CDs synthesized from LMWC and SF mixtures exhibit two absorption peaks. A peak at 275 nm due to π-π* transition of the CC group and a broad peak between 320 and 350 due to the n-π* transition of the CO group (Gu et al., 2019; C. Zhu et al., 2012).

The fluorescence spectra of the CDs synthesized with different ratios of LMWC and SF were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm, and the emission was measured from 360 to 650 and is displayed in Fig. 1(C). An increase in the fluorescence intensity was observed upon increasing the SF content, indicating the successful nitrogen doping of the CDs. A marginal red-shift (~7 nm) upon increasing the ratio of SF was seen. This can be attributed to the introduction of oxygen and nitrogen-containing groups on the surface of CDs due to the higher concentration of SF, which may induce O/N-related defects, varying the HOMO-LUMO gap. When these defects states return to HOMO, shifting of the bands occurs as described in the literature (Chen et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2017). The intensity corresponding to the ratio of 1:1.5 was maximum; hence we have taken CDs (1:1.5) for our further studies.

The emission spectra of CDs (1:1.5) were further analyzed by varying the excitation wavelength from 290 to 400 nm, as shown in Fig. 1(D). It was visible that the emission spectra were highly dependent on the excitation wavelength. The emission intensity increased upon increasing the excitation wavelength from 290 to 350 but gets significantly reduced and red-shifted at an excitation wavelength of 400 nm, as - been reported in other synthesized CDs earlier (Zhao et al., 2017). This can be due to the quantum confinement effect (Zhang et al., 2014). Thus, the fluorescence properties of the material can be tuned by doping- and varying the excitation wavelength.

The quantum yield (φ) of the different ratios of CDs was determined using fluorescein as the reference dye, having φ value of 0.92 in 0.1 N NaOH (Magde et al., 2002). The φ was determined using the following equation (Radwan-pragłowska & Bogdał, 2019).
φ
x
=
φ r
A r
A x
E x
E r
η x
η r
2
where, φ is the relative fluorescence quantum yield; A is the absorbance or optical density (OD) at the excitation wavelength; E is the integrated fluorescence intensity (calculated by the trapezoidal rule using MATLAB); ƞ is the refractive index of the solvent; Subscript ‘x’ and ‘r’ refers to the unknown and reference respectively. The φ of the CDs prepared from blank LMWC and SF was 16% and 44%, respectively. In comparison, the φ of the CDs obtained from LMWC/SF blends were 39%, 45%, and 66%. Thus, an increase in relative φ can be seen in the CDs formulated using the mixtures than CDs prepared from the pristine polymers indicating a synergistic effect of LMWC/SF blends. Moreover, there was an increase in the relative φ with SF content due to nitrogen doping. Table 1 showcases the values of φ of our synthesized CDs along with the φ of the reported CDs synthesized by LMWC and SF using different methods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of physical properties of CDs

ce:section.ce:para: The size of the CDs (1:1.5) was evaluated by FETEM analysis. From the FETEM micrograph in Fig. 2 (A), it is evident that the CDs were quasi-spherical in shape with a size of 3 ± 1.5 nm. The lattice spacing of 0.32 nm is obtained from high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) and inverse fast fourier transform (IFFT) analysis, as shown in Fig. 2(B). The size distribution histogram plot of the CDs demonstrated in Fig. 2(C) was done using ImageJ, which offers a very narrow particle size distribution.

The XRD pattern of pure LMWC exhibits two diffraction peaks at 2θ =10° and 2θ =20° due to its amorphous and crystalline structure, respectively (Duan et al., 2008), as shown in Fig. S2 (supplementary data). Similarly, the XRD pattern of pure SF displays two diffraction peaks at around 20.4° and 24.4° due to β-sheet spacing of 4.3 Å and 3.6 Å, respectively (Nogueira et al., 2009; Whittaker et al., 2014) as in Fig. S3 (supplementary data). While upon carbonizing, the crystallinity of both the biopolymer diminishes, and a broad amorphous peak appears at 2θ = 22.14° as shown in Fig. 2(D), which is attributed to highly disordered carbon atoms in the CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of formation of BT-CDs and drug loading
ce:section.ce:para: FTIR spectroscopy was done to check the biotin conjugation and drug attachment. The FTIR spectrum of CDs (1:1.5) is displayed in Fig. 3 (A). A broad peak centered at 3293 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching and in-plane bending vibrations of the OH and NH groups. The peaks at 1654 cm−1, 1534 cm-1, and 1459 cm−1 correspond to the CO stretching, NH bending, and CN bending vibrations. The resulting FTIR peaks indicate the presence of hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups on the surface of the CDs (Ko et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2016). The FTIR spectrum of BT-NHS is presented in Fig. 3(B). The peak at 3246 cm−1 is for NH stretching, 2925 cm−1 is for CH stretching, 1831 is due to symmetric stretching of the succinimide CO, 1701 cm−1 is due asymmetric stretching of the succinimide CO, 1475, and 1369 cm−1 is for CH and NH, 1202 cm−1 is for ester NO and 1072 cm−1 for ester CO (Błauż et al., 2021; Chauhan & Singh, 2011). The FTIR spectra of pure biotin and NHS is presented in the supporting information. In the FTIR spectrum of BT-CDs in Fig. 3(C), the peak at 3246 cm−1 is due to NH stretching, 2925 cm−1 is for CH stretching, and 1024 cm−1 is for ester NO of biotin. A sharp peak has originated at 1558 cm−1, corresponding to NH bending and 1689 cm−1 for CO stretching vibration, confirming the new amide bond (-CONH-) formation between the CDs and biotin (Yao et al., 2017). The FTIR spectrum of pure 5-FU is presented in Fig. 3(D), which shows the characteristic peaks at 1328 cm−1, 1648 cm−1, 1512 cm−1, 1431 cm−1, 1232 cm−1, and 798 cm−1 due to NH stretching, CO stretching, NH bending, CN stretching, CF stretching, and CH bending, respectively. The FTIR spectrum of FU-BT-CDs displayed in Fig. 3(E) shows peaks of BT-CDs along with the peaks of 5-FU at 1416 cm−1, 1237 cm-1, and 798 cm−1, confirming the binding of 5-FU (Li et al., 2011; Olukman, 2012).

The formation of BT-NHS was further investigated by electrospray ionization-mass spectrometry (ESI-MS). The mass spectrum is presented in Fig. S12 (supplementary data). The spectrum shows peaks of [M + H]+ at 342.11 and [2 M + H]+ at 683.21, which is a clear indication of the formation of BT-NHS (molecular weight = 341.10). We have also checked the morphology of the drug-loaded BT-CDs by AFM, as presented in Fig. S13 (supplementary data).

The EE % and DL % of 5-FU were evaluated for all the CDs formulated using the varying ratios of LMWC to SF. Table S1 (supplementary data) demonstrates the EE % and DL % values of the CDs. The CDs prepared from pristine LMWC showed an average EE % of 87% and DL % of 28%. But, upon addition of SF, the average EE % increased to 90%, 95%, and 97%, for LMWC: SF ratio of 1:0.6, 1:1 and 1:1.5 respectively. While a minor increase in the DL % was observed with 29%, 34%, and 35% for LMWC: SF ratio of 1:0.6, 1:1, and 1:1.5, respectively. The amplified value of the EE % and DL % values might be due to the increased functionality of CDs upon SF addition which enhances its interaction with 5-FU.

The in vitro drug release from the FU-BT-CDs was carried out in a PBS buffer of 7.4 pH. The plot of the percentage drug release vs. time is shown in Fig. 4 (A). The CDs exhibited an initial burst release of 25% until 2 h because of the migration of loosely bound drugs on the surface to the release medium. This was followed by sustained release of 81% of the drugs until 36 h by diffusion.

The cell viability assay of MCF-7, HeLa, and HEK-293 cell lines treated with varying concentrations of (a) CDs, (b) 5-FU, (c) FU-CDs, and (d) FU-BT-CDs are presented in Fig. 4(B), (C), and (D), respectively. In all the cell lines, pure CDs showed high cell viability. This elucidates the biocompatible nature of the CDs. In other treatment groups, a dose-dependent decrease in cell viability was witnessed. The cytotoxicity of the biotin-conjugated material (FU-BT-CDs) was higher than the non-conjugated ones in the cancerous cell lines MCF-7 and HeLa, which exhibits a positive (+) degree of biotin expression. However, there was minimal difference in cytotoxicity between non-conjugated and biotin-conjugated material in the normal cells HEK-293 with a negative (−) degree of biotin expression. The IC50 values of all the synthesized materials in the respective cell lines are presented in Table S2 (supplementary data). The IC50 values in HEK-293 are much higher in comparison to MCF-7 and HeLa. The IC50 of FU-BT-CDs in HEK-293 is 138.10 μM, while in MCF-7 and HeLa, it is 28.19 μM and 38.17 μM, respectively. This shows the low cytotoxicity of the material in normal cell lines and increased target specificity due to biotin conjugation. Our synthesized material has shown better efficiency in MCF-7 cell lines than various earlier reported systems for 5-FU such as nanocomposites of chitosan/gold, chitosan/silver, chitosan/silver/MWCNTs (Nivethaa et al., 2015; Nivethaa et al., 2016); folate-conjugated chitosan gold nanoparticles (Akinyelu & Singh, 2019), PEGylated nanoparticles (Abd-Rabou et al., 2018), which had shown very higher IC50 values.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Confocal imaging to study the cellular uptake and specificity
ce:section.ce:para: The cell imaging was performed to check the efficiency of the synthesized CDs as a cell imaging tool and for the visual evaluation of targeting efficiency. Fig. 5 (A) and (B) display the cell imaging data of CDs and BT-CDs, respectively, in MCF-7 cell lines. The figures demonstrated successful internalization of the CDs and BT-CDs, as evident from the bright blue fluorescence inside the cells. Comparing their localization, it was found that CDs are amassed in the cytoplasm only. In contrast, BT-CDs are distributed in the entire cell, primarily in the nucleus after their uptake by the cancer cells. This is due to the biotin receptors overexpressing on MCF-7 cell lines, which induced receptor-mediated endocytosis. The high fluorescence property of the CDs, along with biotin conjugation, which emphasized the improved targeting property, makes the synthesized conjugated material an ideal choice for targeted drug delivery and cell imaging. The image of untreated cells is shown in Fig. S14 (supplementary data).

Fig. S15(A) and (B) (supplementary data) show the cell imaging data of BT-CDs in HEK-293 and MCF-7 cell lines, respectively. It was evident from the confocal images that there was increased cellular uptake of the biotin-conjugated CDs in the cancer cell line (MCF-7) with positive biotin expression compared to the non-cancerous cell line (HEK-293) with biotin negative (−) expression. This shows the specificity of the biotin-conjugated material (BT-CDs) towards cancer cells.

CDs can be a promising nanomaterial for drug delivery. It is highly non-toxic, easy to synthesize, cost-effective, possesses many functional groups, and has fluorescence properties. Herein, highly fluorescent, tunable, primary amine-functionalized CDs have been successfully synthesized using LMWC and SF blend. The LMWC acts as the primary carbon and functionalizing source, while SF act as the nitrogen doping source. It was observed that the CDs formulated using the blend possess high fluorescence intensity and quantum yield compared to the CDs prepared from blank LMWC and SF, showing a synergistic effect. In addition, upon increasing the SF content in the blend, there was an enhancement in the fluorescence properties due to doping. The CDs were quasi-spherical in shape with a size of 3 ± 1.5 nm and narrow particle size distribution. The powder XRD data revealed that upon carbonization, the crystallinity of the biopolymers had been drastically reduced, and the CDs were amorphous in nature. The cancer cell targeting moiety, biotin, has been successfully conjugated to the CD, and the 5-FU has also been loaded with a very high encapsulation efficiency. The MTT assay of the conjugated CDs exhibited increased cytotoxicity in cancer cell lines, MCF-7 (IC50 = 28.19) and HeLa (IC50 = 38.18), as compared to normal HEK-296 cell lines (IC50 = 138.10). The cell imaging on MCF-7 cell lines showed bright blue fluorescence with increased internalization of the conjugated CDs compared to normal cell lines due to biotin receptor-mediated endocytosis. The high water solubility, drug encapsulating efficiency, fluorescence properties, and target specificity proves the efficiency of the synthesized biotin-conjugated CDs as drug delivery carrier and cell-imaging tool.

Himali Horo: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Muktashree Saha: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Himadree Das: Investigation, Formal analysis. Bishnupada Mandal: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Lal Mohan Kundu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.
